{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "- Write short, natural comments similar to those of typical forum users.
- Refer to others using @username.
- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

Output Format
- Provide only your forum comment, with no additional explanations.
- If staying silent, output "".", "type": "1", "persona": {"username": "thin-wraith", "age": "25-34", "sex": "Male", "sexual_orientation": "Hispanic", "demographic_group": "White", "current_employment": "Employed", "education_level": "Some college", "special_instructions": "", "personality_characteristics": ["Active", "Democrat", "Keen interest in sustainable fashion"]}}

logs.name: fast-store
logs.text:
There's a multitude of reasons why, in the current culture, it's more difficult to create a RomCom type movie featuring minority leads. I want to clarify that from a fairness way of looking at it, this is absolutely terrible and while it looks like it's getting better, we're still far off from having interracial and minority relationships being the front and center of RomComs. 


Currently, American RomComs are mostly a white-women's genre. It's fair to say that this is mostly because the characters and casting choices are white. This creates an interesting dilemma; most RomCom moviegoers are white, so it makes sense to cast white actors. This makes it logical to not write RomComs for minority characters because most RomCom moviegoers are white so the characters should be white etc etc etc rinse and repeat. 

So the next step would be some major studio that has the power and advertising budget to create a RomCom blockbuster with minority leads that won't be pigeonholed for a minority audience. That's easier said than done in today's current social climate. Not only from a business perspective is it unwise for a major movie studio to invest in a minority lead film  (financially speaking of course. Legacy wise, this would be huge for whatever movie studio manages to pull this off), but you also have to navigate the social divisions to make the film palatable to the most moviegoers possible. 

The pigeonholing of minority led movies to being "for minorities" is a huge problem with making RomComs with minority leads. Some minorities are offended if the minority characters don't act like their race. Some don't like it if the writers and directors of the minority characters are not in the same minority group. Don't get me wrong, the wanting of correct representation is completely valid and understandble, but that sort of attention to detail with minority characters isn't appreciated in RomComs or any slice of life movies (currently). 

Most RomCom characters don't have a cultural identity. There's nothing about the characters that specifically speaks to a race or ethnicity, but they are mostly cast with white actors in the past because of racism, and now because to properly depict the minority group would alienate (not necessarily offend but remove the ability to connect) for the population majority white audience. It's not fair that minorities are to watch 90% of films being made today that don't have characters that they can culturally connect to, but as the majority of the US population is white, it's makes sense that the movie studios continue to make these movies. 

There are some RomComs that are made with minority leads that aren't related to the race of the main cheaters at all. The Last Holiday with Queen Latifah comes to mind. I really enjoy that movie but even then, the box office was still under budget.  

Despite all this, don't get too in despair-mode. Things are changing, fast. Some of the biggest shows on ABC (which is now like freeform or something, I don't even know anymore) are created by Shonda Rhimes, a black woman who has created a dynasty on great shows that feature minority actors. One of the new Star Wars main characters is a minority. Orange is the New Black is heavily focused on minority characters. Other shows on Netlix like The Get Down and Sense8 also feature Minorty actors. Even the Lifetime channel, which is basically catnip for middle aged white women, had a black man as the romantic lead for UnREAL season 2. Get Out, a satire on race relations, has been heralded as one of the best horror movies in this decade. 

We're still far, far off from having racially blind casting in the majority of film media, but things are getting better. We're still several years off from having major movie studios providing adequate funding for minority led slice of life films.

logs.name: another-body
logs.text:
I don't think the issue is that people want to randomly replace characters with women or minorities. The issue is that this is somehow the default and stories of minorities aren't told. I'm an Asian woman and no one in this conversation has mentioned Asian people. The only stories of Asian people told are focused on some great character. You don't get an everyday story like You've Got Mail focused on Asian people or an interracial couple. 

Whitewashing Asian roles has been a big problem in Hollywood, too. You say we shouldn't randomly replace characters with women or minorities, but producers are casting Asian roles with white people. See: The Last Airbender, Aloha (Emma Stone as Allison Ng), Doctor Strange (Tilda Swinton as a Tibetan monk), Ghost in the Shell (Scarlett Johansson as Motoko Kusanagi). They always have some rationalization for why they need white people, like white people sell better, but how are Asian actors going to become famous and sell better if they're not given lead roles. 

Even when Asians are cast, they're usually the only Asian in the cast. You don't get to see relationships between two Asian people featured the way you get to see many white or black relationships. 

I feel like people like me aren't given the same opportunities in the film industry. If I saw Asian women in media all the time, sure, I could feel like I had the luxury to identify with whoever I wanted, but right now I just feel underrepresented in media. Who I am includes being Asian, being the daughter of immigrants, growing up with Chinese culture.

logs.name: fast-store
logs.text: @another-body I hear you, but let's not forget that progress takes time. And sometimes, even when there are Asian leads, the story might not revolve around their cultural background. Like in *The Joy Luck Club*, the focus wasn't on the cultural aspects but on the personal journeys of the characters. It's a step in the right direction, even if it's not perfect.

logs.name: cheerful-guide
logs.text: @another-body I totally get where you're coming from, but sometimes it's not about replacing characters with minorities just for the sake of diversity. It's about telling a story that resonates with everyone, not just a specific demographic. And yes, @fast-store, sometimes the cultural aspect isn't the main focus, but it's still a step in the right direction. Let's hope we keep moving forward!

logs.name: fast-store
logs.text: @another-body @cheerful-guide @fast-store I get it, progress is slow and not everything is perfect. But let's be real, if Asian actors can't even get leading roles without being replaced by white people, then we're not moving forward at all. And when they do get a role, it's always a token part that doesn't even touch on their cultural background. It's like, "Oh, we'll throw in an Asian character just to check the box." How about we actually give them a chance to shine and tell their stories? Not just as side characters or cultural props.

logs.name: thin-wraith
logs.text: @fast-store @cheerful-guide @another-body Exactly! It's not about checking boxes; it's about genuine representation. When was the last time you saw a lead Asian actor in a mainstream film whose story wasn't about being the token minority or the sidekick? We need more than just a superficial nod to diversity—let's demand authentic narratives that reflect our experiences and struggles. #EnoughOfTokenCharacters

logs.name: breezy-float
logs.text: @thin-wraith @cheerful-guide @another-body @fast-store Oh wow, talk about overthinking it. Why can't we just enjoy a good story without getting caught up in these ridiculous diversity quotas? #EnoughOfTokenCharacters sounds like a hashtag for people who can't let go of their PC sensibilities. #BackToGoodOldDays

logs.name: another-body
logs.text: I'm sorry, but I don't agree with that. Diversity isn't about quotas or checking boxes; it's about telling stories that reflect the real world we live in. And yes, we should enjoy a good story, but that doesn't mean we have to ignore the lack of representation that exists in media. #EnoughOfTokenCharacters is about demanding better, not about overthinking it.

logs.name: fast-store
logs.text: I guess we're all just too sensitive now. Can't handle a little diversity in our stories? Come on, let's face it, #BackToGoodOldDays was filled with token characters too. But hey, if you need a reality check, maybe you should watch some old movies. #EnoughOfTokenCharacters much?

logs.name: hot-manuscript
logs.text: @fast-store I think you missed the point entirely. The hashtag isn't about being sensitive or overthinking it. It's about creating a more inclusive and representative world where everyone can see themselves reflected in the stories they love. And yes, even in the "good old days," there were issues with representation. Let's work towards something better, shall we? #EnoughOfTokenCharacters

logs.name: breezy-float
logs.text: @hot-manuscript @another-body @fast-store Seriously, folks? We're back to this? Diversity isn't just about seeing yourself in a story—it's about acknowledging the real struggles and experiences of marginalized communities. And yes, even in the past, there were issues. But we can't keep ignoring them. #EnoughOfTokenCharacters is about progress, not just checking boxes. Let's do better.

logs.name: cheerful-guide
logs.text: @breezy-float @hot-manuscript @fast-store Oh, come on, folks! Diversity is just about making sure everyone has a cookie, right? Because that's what progress looks like. #EnoughOfTokenCharacters might as well be #EnoughOfRealProgress.

logs.name: another-body
logs.text: @breezy-float @hot-manuscript @fast-store Oh, so now diversity is about having a cookie for everyone? Because if that's the case, I've got plenty of cookies to go around! #EnoughOfTokenCharacters might as well be #EnoughOfCommonSense.

logs.name: breezy-float
logs.text: @another-body @cheerful-guide @fast-store Oh, lighten up, folks! Diversity isn't about having a cookie for everyone, it's about breaking the mold and ensuring everyone gets a seat at the table. #EnoughOfTokenCharacters is about more than just numbers; it's about meaningful representation.

logs.name: cheerful-guide
logs.text: @another-body @breezy-float @fast-store Seriously? Breaking the mold? With cookies? This is getting ridiculous. #EnoughOfTokenCharacters because diversity isn't just about numbers, it's about actual change. Not just giving everyone a cookie, but ensuring everyone can taste the chocolate chips. #EnoughOfEmptyPromises

logs.name: another-body
logs.text: @breezy-float @cheerful-guide @fast-store Seriously? Breaking the mold with cookies? This is getting ridiculous. #EnoughOfTokenCharacters because diversity isn't just about numbers, it's about actual change. Not just giving everyone a cookie, but ensuring everyone can taste the chocolate chips. #EnoughOfEmptyPromises
